title: Are physical activity interventions equally effective in adolescents of low and high socio-economic status (SES): results from the European Teenage project
abstract: The aim was to study whether physical activity (PA) interventions in European teenagers are equally effective in adolescents of low versus high socio-economic status (SES). Based on a systematic review (Project TEENAGE), three school-based studies for secondary analyses were selected. SES stratified analyses were run in: (i) a Belgian multi-component intervention, (ii) a French multi-component intervention and (iii) a Belgian computer-tailored education trial. Results of the secondary analyses showed that no overall significant differences between low and high SES groups were found, but some interesting specific effects were revealed. Results from the first study showed an increase in objective PA in the low SES group (P 5 0.015) compared with no significant effects in the high SES group. In the second study, larger effects were found in adolescents of high SES (increase of 11 min day 21 P < 0.001), compared with adolescents of lower SES (increase of 7 min day 21 , P 5 0.02) at the longer term. The third study showed a positive effect on school-related PA in adolescents of high SES (P < 0.05) and on leisure time transportation in adolescents of low SES (P < 0.05). To conclude, we were not able to show a significant widening or narrowing of inequalities in European adolescents.
text: The aim was to study whether physical activity (PA) interventions in European teenagers are equally effective in adolescents of low versus high socio-economic status (SES). Based on a systematic review (Project TEENAGE), three school-based studies for secondary analyses were selected. SES stratified analyses were run in: (i) a Belgian multi-component intervention, (ii) a French multi-component intervention and (iii) a Belgian computer-tailored education trial. Results of the secondary analyses showed that no overall significant differences between low and high SES groups were found, but some interesting specific effects were revealed. Results from the first study showed an increase in objective PA in the low SES group (P 5 0.015) compared with no significant effects in the high SES group. In the second study, larger effects were found in adolescents of high SES (increase of 11 min day 21 P < 0.001), compared with adolescents of lower SES (increase of 7 min day 21 , P 5 0.02) at the longer term. The third study showed a positive effect on school-related PA in adolescents of high SES (P < 0.05) and on leisure time transportation in adolescents of low SES (P < 0.05). To conclude, we were not able to show a significant widening or narrowing of inequalities in European adolescents.
main_text: Introduction International studies have shown that levels of physical activity (PA) tend to decline rapidly in adolescence [1] [2] [3] . As a result, a large percentage of adolescents do not achieve the public health guidelines of 60 min day À1 PA of moderate to vigorous intensity [4] [5] [6] . A specific at-risk group for low levels of PA are adolescents of parents with a lower socio-economic status (SES). A recent study showed a clear inverse relationship between PA [specifically moderate to vigorous activity (MVPA)] and parents' SES in 11-, 13-and 15year-old students in the majority of 32 countries participating in the Health Behaviour in Schoolaged Children (HBSC) survey 2001/2002 [7] . In addition, a recent review by Ferreira et al. [8] showed that mother's educational level and family income were consistently and inversely associated with PA levels among adolescents. Effective interventions are needed to increase the level of PA in European adolescents, particularly in adolescents with a lower SES. We conducted recently a review within the European project TEENAGE to identify interventions targeting PA in European teenagers. The review had a specific focus on Europe, as Europe is different from other continents with regard to the structure and organization of education, the environmental features at home and in the neighbourhood and therefore perhaps also for the effectiveness of school-based interventions. Twenty relevant studies were identified in this review, of which 15 were delivered through the school setting, 1 was conducted within a community setting, 3 were delivered in primary care and 1 was delivered through the internet [9] . A main finding of this review was that school-based promotion programmes in Europe showed at least short-term effects on PA levels in the general adolescent population under investigation. The available evidence for effectiveness in other settings was rather limited. The review also revealed that no information was available in Europe about the differential effects of these interventions across adolescents with a higher or lower SES. Thus, it remains unknown whether the introduction of effective interventions will equally affect adolescents with different SES or, as is often believed, will widen inequalities due to a higher effectiveness in higher SES groups. A second purpose of the TEENAGE project [10] was the identification of effective European interventions that could further provide information on this issue through secondary SES stratified analyses. The aim of the present study was to explore the effectiveness of effective European interventions promoting PA in adolescents from a lower and higher SES.MethodsSelection of included studies Based on the systematic review, the original authors of eight moderate to high quality European studies with clear overall effects were contacted as potential candidates for the secondary analyses [9] . The authors of two studies did not answer, two other studies did not have an SES indicator and the authors of the fifth study were still conducting primary anal-yses. This resulted in three studies that were selected. The original authors conducted the SES stratified analyses using the same protocol for data analysis as they used for their study on overall intervention effects. All three studies (two from Belgium and one from France) were school-based programs to increase PA levels in adolescents. Two of them were multi-component school-based interventions combining a health education component in the classroom with changes in the school environment [11, 12] . The third study was a smaller, more specific and shorter study, evaluating a computer-tailored intervention during classes to encourage a physically active lifestyle [13] . Belgian and French school systems are very comparable: schools are funded by the government, parents only pay a small amount per year, low and high SES children are mixed within schools, quality between schools does not differ a lot, children attend school from about 8.30 a.m. in the morning till 4.00 p.m. in the evening and all children follow compulsory physical education lessons.Study 1: Belgian multi-component intervention The study by Haerens et al. [11] was conducted in Belgian middle schools. A random sample of 15 schools was randomly assigned to (a) an intervention with parental support (5 schools), (b) an intervention alone (5 schools) and (c) a control condition (5 schools). A sample of 2840 seventh and eight graders (11-15 year olds) participated in the study (mean 13.1 6 0.8 years old). Response rate of the adolescents within the schools was 95%. Schools changed their physical environment by creating more opportunities to be physically active during breaks, at noon or after school hours. The content of the PAs varied and noncompetitive activities were included in order to reach less skilled students. Every school received an intervention box with sports materials. The health education component consisted of personal computerized PA advice for every student minimal once each school year. In 5 of the 10 intervention schools, parents were invited for an interactive meeting, information was also communicated through home correspondence and all parents received a free CD with the adult computer-tailored intervention [14] to complete at I. De Bourdeaudhuij et al. home. The intervention was implemented during one school year and effects were measured after 1 and after 2 years. A detailed description of the design, methods, intervention content and general effects can be found elsewhere [11, 15] .Study 2: French multi-component intervention The study by Simon et al. [16, 17] was a multicomponent school-based intervention involving both the family and the wider community [12] . The Intervention Centred on Adolescents' Physical activity and Sedentary behaviour (ICAPS) was designed to cover four school years. The target population was a sample of 954 students of middle schools (aged 11.7 6 0.6 years) from four pairs of schools randomly selected in eastern France, after stratifying for lower and higher SES regions. In each pair, intervention status was randomised at school-level. Response rate of the adolescents within the schools was 92%. The intervention involved schools, families and the community and aimed at increasing access to attractive activities during breaks and after-school hours, at encouraging social support and at providing environmental conditions that enable PA. Strategies that were used were as follows: increased accessibility through adapted times and places, open participation, emphasis on fun, meeting with others, absence of competitive aspects and reducing usual barriers to PA. A detailed description of the design, methods, intervention content and general effects can be found elsewhere [16, 17] .Study 3: Belgian computer-tailored intervention The study by Haerens et al. [13] was the evaluation of a 1-hour computer-tailored intervention offered during class-time in seventh graders (13.2 6 0.7 years). Two classes from 10 randomly selected schools were assigned to the intervention (computer-tailored intervention, n = 139) or control (no-intervention, n = 142) condition. Response rate of the adolescents within the schools was 86%. After completing online questionnaires in the computer classes at school, the personal PA advice was immediately shown on the screen and could be printed. The advice was based on health behaviour change theories and included normative feedback, which related students' PA levels to the PA guidelines, and tailored feedback about their attitudes, self-efficacy, social support, knowledge, perceived benefits and barriers related to their PA. The effects were measured 3 months after the intervention session. A detailed description of the design, methods, intervention content and general effects can be found elsewhere [13] . Measures Study 1: Belgian multi-component intervention SES of the adolescent was estimated using SES of the family (self-reported by adolescents) by classifying the occupation of the father and mother into white-and blue-collar jobs [11, 18] . High SES adolescents were those with at least one parent with a white-collar job (n = 888 high SES, n = 1793 low SES). PA levels were determined using the validated Flemish Physical Activity Questionnaire [19] . Total PA level (minutes per day) was determined as a result of three components: (i) school related PA, (ii) leisure time sports and (iii) leisure time active transportation. Questions addressing frequency and duration of activities were completed. PA levels were also assessed by accelerometers in a random subsample of 183 students. Students were instructed to wear the accelerometer above the right hipbone and underneath the clothes for four weekdays and two weekend days. Cut-points of Puyau [20] were used to determine minutes of activity of light or moderate to vigorous (MVPA) intensity. Study 2: French multi-component intervention SES of the adolescent was estimated using the highest occupational category of either parent, which was classified as low or high [16, 17] . High SES adolescents were those with at least one parent with a high occupation classification [n = 364 high SES (executive, top-management, professional and intermediate categories), n = 590 low SES (low service professions and blue collars)]. PA levels were assessed with a validated questionnaire adapted PA interventions in low and high SES groups from the 'Modifiable Activity Questionnaire for Adolescents' (MAQ) [21, 22] . Participation in PA during the past year in and outside sports clubs was reported by giving information on the weekly frequency and usual duration of each session. Additional questions not present in the original MAQ about commuting between home and school were also included.Study 3: Belgian computer-tailored intervention Educational level of the adolescent him/herself was used to assign the adolescents to low or high SES groups [13] . Adolescents following general education (focus on theoretical expertise) were categorized as high SES and adolescents following technical or vocational education (focus on practical skills) were categorized as low SES (n = 185 high SES, n = 95 low SES). PA levels were determined using the same validated Flemish Physical Activity Questionnaire as in Study 1 [19] . The same outcome measures were computed and used in this study. A detailed description of this measure is outlined above under Study 1.Statistical analyses Study 1: Belgian multi-component intervention Analyses similar to previously published analyses [11] were conducted separately for the high and low SES group [11, 15] . Linear mixed models on post values of PA were conducted with condition entered as a factor. Because of the gender disparity across conditions, gender and gender by condition interactions were entered as factors in the models. School was nested within condition to take into account clustering at the school level. All analyses were adjusted for baseline values and age. Intention-to-treat analyses were conducted by imputing baseline measures. Post hoc tests analyses were conducted to determine differences in PA changes between the conditions.Study 2: French multi-component intervention Analyses similar to previous published analyses [17] were conducted separately for high and low SES groups [16, 17] . The analysis of outcomes used mixed linear models taking into account the cluster randomization and the repeated individual data over time, by means of PROC MIXED. Random effects were schools within baseline stratification and individuals within schools. Main effects were intervention, time and intervention-by-time interaction. Fixed effects were baseline stratification, gender, sexual maturity, initial weight status, baseline participation to sports-club and their interactions with time. Adjusted least-square means and differences across groups of their within-group changes over time are presented with their 95% confidence intervals. Possible heterogeneity of the intervention's effect was tested. No interaction with gender was found.Study 3: Belgian computer-tailored intervention Similar to previous analyses [13] , analyses were executed on the four outcomes. Linear mixed models on post-intervention measures of PA, with 'condition'; 'gender' and 'compliance with the PA guidelines' as between subjects' factors, were used to explore intervention effects on PA separately for students engaging in general education and students engaging in technical-vocational education. Class was nested within condition to take possible clustering within classes into account. Analyses were adjusted for baseline values of PA and age. Only the results of the stratified analyses are reported in the results section. Before executing the separate analyses for low and high SES groups, interaction effects were tested between SES and intervention condition on PA outcomes in all three studies. None of the interaction effects was significant (all P > 0.10). The overall results of the different studies can be found in the original manuscripts and also in our review [9] .Results As the interaction effects were not significant, we were not able to show significant differences between socio-economic groups. However, stratified analyses showed some differences between low The pre-to post-intervention values for self-reported PA and for accelerometer-based PA for the high SES group are presented at the top of Table I . Self-reported total PA levels significantly increased in the intervention group with parental support (+6 min day À1 ), whereas in the intervention-alone group and the control group decreases were found (À4.5 min day À1 ). School-related PA increased significantly more in the intervention group with parental support (+8 min day À1 ) when compared with the intervention-alone group (+4 min day À1 ) and the control group (+1 min day À1 ). No significant effects were found for the accelerometer data [11] .Low SES groups The intervention effects for the low SES group are presented at the bottom of Table I . School-related PA increased significantly more in the intervention group with parental support (+6 min day À1 ) when compared with the control group (À1 min day À1 ). There was a trend for a significant difference between the intervention alone and the control group (F = 3.2, P < 0.1). In the intervention-alone group school-related PA increased with on average 4 min day À1 , while in the control group, no changes were found. Self-reported leisure time active transportation decreased significantly less in the interventionalone group (À3 min day À1 ) when compared with the control group (À5 min day À1 ). Intervention effects by accelerometers also showed significances in the low SES group. For MVPA, there was a significant difference between the intervention group with parental support and the control group (F = 6.2, P < 0.05). MVPA significantly increased with on average 6 min day À1 in the intervention group with parental support, while it decreased with almost 9 min day À1 in the control group. There was a trend for a significant difference between the intervention-alone group and the control group (F = 2.8, P < 0.1). In the intervention-alone group, MVPA remained stable, while it decreased with on average 9 min day À1 in the control group.Study 2: French multi-component intervention High SES group. Supervised PA (either in a sports club or through ICAPS activities) increased in intervention students, while it was stable in the controls, with a difference across groups of the 3-and 4-year within-group changes of about 12 min day À1 (P < 0.01) Table II . The slight increase observed for active commuting to and from school was similar for both groups [16, 17] . Low SES group. Supervised PA also increased significantly in intervention low SES students, while it slightly decreased in the controls. A difference across groups at 1 year (+8 min day À1 , P < 0.001), 2 years (+5 min day À1 , P = 0.03), 3 years (+7 min day À1 , P < 0.01) and 4 years (+5 min day À1 , P = 0.07) was found. The slight increase observed for active commuting to and from school was similar for both groups (Table II ).Study 3: Belgian computer-tailored intervention High SES group. The pre and post-intervention values of self-reported PA are presented at the top of Table III for students engaging in general education. There were no significant interaction effects between condition and gender nor between condition and students meeting or not meeting the guidelines of 60 min of MVPA. There were no significant intervention effects on total PA, leisure time sports and leisure time active transport (all F < 1.4). Among students engaging in general education, there was a significant main effect of condition on school-related PA (F = 4.0, P < 0.05). Schoolrelated PA increased on average 4.5 min day À1 in the intervention group, while no increases were found in the control group [13] . Low SES group. The intervention effects are presented at the bottom of Table III for students engaging in technical and vocational education. There were no significant interaction effects between condition and gender nor between condition and PA interventions in low and high SES groups students meeting or not meeting the guidelines of 60 min of MVPA. There were no significant intervention effects on total PA, school-based PA and leisure time sports (all F < 1.4). Among students engaging in technical-vocational education, there was a significant main effect of condition on leisure time active transportation (F = 4.5, P < 0.05). Although students in the intervention group already had higher baseline levels of active transportation in leisure time, leisure time active transportation increased with on average 3 min day À1 in the intervention group, while it increased significantly less (2 min day À1 ) in the control group.Discussion The aim of the present manuscript was to study whether PA interventions in European teenagers are equally effective in promoting a physically active lifestyle in low versus high SES adolescents. Secondary SES stratified were run on 3 high quality interventions that already reported positive effects in the overall sample. In general, the secondary analyses showed that the interventions reached similar effects in low and in high SES adolescents. We were not able to show a significant widening or narrowing of inequalities. Results from the first study, the Belgian Multicomponent Intervention, revealed that effects were in the same order for low SES adolescents compared with high SES adolescents. In high SES ado-lescents, the intervention succeeded in increasing total PA levels mainly through PA at school or to and from school (640 min week À1 ). In low SES adolescents, the same increases in school-related PA were found (630 min week À1 ), but they were also supplemented with effects on leisure time active transportation. An important finding was that the self-reported increases in PA were only confirmed with the objective accelerometer-based activity levels (increase of 40 min week À1 MVPA) in low SES adolescents and not in high SES adolescents. Results from the second Multicomponent intervention, the French ICAPS study, also revealed effects in high and low SES adolescents. In high SES adolescents, the intervention showed clear effects on supervised PA and sports inside and outside school. After 1 and 2 years of intervention, only small non-significant differences between intervention and control groups were found. However, 3 and 4 years after baseline, a favourable change of about 80 min week À1 was found in high SES adolescents. In low SES adolescents, clear intervention effects were also found on supervised PA. Effects were found in all 4 years of the intervention, resulting in a favourable change between 35 min week À1 and 55 min week À1 at each of the 4 years of follow-up. This difference between a 80 min week À1 increase in high SES adolescents compared with a 45 min week À1 increase in low SES adolescents is noteworthy. Results from this French study showed that especially at the longer Least-square means and differences across groups of their within-group changes over time (95% CI) obtained from linear mixed models adjusting for gender, sexual maturity, socioeconomic status, initial weight status, baseline participation to sports-club, baseline stratification and their interactions with time. b p are given for intervention by time interaction. c I= intervention group; C= control group.I. De Bourdeaudhuij et al. 126 term, after 4 years, stronger effects were found in higher SES groups. We can only speculate on why effects were stronger in the longer term for the high SES group compared with the low SES group. One possible explanation could be the increased age of the adolescents at the follow-ups. It is possible that effects of interventions between low and high SES groups are more comparable in younger adolescents (11-12 year olds at baseline) than in older ones (15-16 year olds). Another explanation could be that low SES adolescents have increased other types of PA (e.g. active transport) and not the structured PA lead by an educator measured in this study. The third study included in this paper was a smaller and short-term computer-tailored education trial. This trial revealed effects on different PA indices in high and low SES adolescents. In high SES adolescents, a positive effect on schoolrelated PA was found after 3 months, whereas in low SES adolescents, a positive effect on leisure time transportation was found. The relative low sample size of this study raises particularly questions on the power to analyse results stratified by SES. This power problem might explain the small number of significant tests in this study. The major finding of this manuscript is that the general belief that interventions might widen inequalities is not automatically evident. Although the differences found between high and low SES groups need to be confirmed in other studies, it is clear that relatively equal effects in both SES groups were shown. The reason for this can be found in the characteristics of the interventions. The first two interventions incorporated wholeof-school approaches including curriculum, policy and environmental strategies. The environmental component of both interventions focussed on increasing PA opportunities at school. They were offered for free or at a very low cost which could have offered a solution for the financial barriers that often exist in low SES adolescents to pay for membership and sports material within regular sports clubs [23, PA interventions in low and high SES groups 24]. In addition, Humbert et al. [24] showed through focus groups with adolescents that next to low costs, also ease of accessibility of PA programs and facilities and the need for high quality equipment and well-maintained facilities are considered to be more important in low SES youth. Low SES youth considered an activity stimulating environment as an important factor for their PA participation, while high SES youth did not. Previous studies in adults [25] [26] [27] but also in adolescents [10, [28] [29] [30] [31] [32] already showed that living in low SES neighbourhoods is related to less opportunities to be physically active. This makes it of utmost importance that low SES adolescents have easy access to high-quality PA facilities and support at school. A second element of the whole-of-school approach was the explicit attention for the educational component in relation with the environmental component. Both interventions focused on awareness raising, increasing self-efficacy, stimulating support among peers and teachers, increasing skills, overcoming barriers, etc. Both interventions put extra emphasis on stimulating an active lifestyle and participation in non-competitive sports rather than stimulating sport competitions in already active pupils. This explicit attention at school for the combination of both psychosocial and environmental determinants of PA may be very fruitful in behaviour change in lower SES adolescents. A potential limitation of previous health education interventions for the purpose of reducing socioeconomic inequalities in health is that it did not take the circumstances in which individuals live into account, which might differ substantially in low and high SES adolescents [10] . Computer-tailored interventions could be an answer for this problem, as each adolescent receives detailed tailored feedback that is adapted to him or her specific context. However, the computer-tailored study included in the present manuscript did show quite small and diverse effects on different PA indices in low and high SES adolescents. Next to the power problem in this study related to the stratified analyses which was already mentioned before, this might also suggest that a computer-tailored approach alone, without an environmental component, does not increase or reduce social inequalities, but is simply not strong enough to yield clear behaviour effects. A recent European review of school-based interventions for the prevention of overweight and obesity in adolescents also showed that especially multicomponent interventions including an educational and an environmental component are promising in changing behaviour habits and overweight [33] . This would plead for always complementing educational efforts with environmental initiatives in promoting PA through the schools [34] . Some study limitations need to be addressed. First, our selection procedure resulted in three school-based studies of which were two from Belgium and one from France. It is clear that these studies are not representative for the whole of Europe nor for the whole of initiatives or strategies developed to increase PA in youth. More diversity in study designs and countries would have been preferable. The fact that these interventions showed effects that were in the same order in high versus low SES adolescents does not automatically reveal its relevance for other countries. Further evaluation is needed before findings of intervention research can be transferred from one country to another [10] . Second, different SES indicators, measures and statistical analyses were used in the three included studies. It was our explicit choice to relate as close as possible to the original outcome measures, statistical analyses and variables used in the original published studies and not to try to make the three studies comparable for the sake of this manuscript. So we gave the autonomy to the original authors to select the appropriate measures and techniques and to execute the secondary analyses faithful to the original design and context of the study. This also means that low and high SES categories do not have the same meaning in the three studies. Third, the three studies were originally not designed and powered to execute the secondary analyses stratified for SES. This was especially problematic in the third study. We tried to circumvent this problem by not only looking at significances but also at changes in mean scores over time in both low and high SES groups. In addition, the reasons for the differences in effects between both SES groups dependent on the outcome variable chosen are hard to explain. More research is warranted here as this may be important for the design of future interventions targeting these groups. To conclude, we think that these secondary analyses are important as they focus on a research question where no primary data are available. Despite of the limitations going together with analysing data in a way they were not originally meant for, we are convinced that these analyses have shed some light on the differences in effectiveness of effective European interventions promoting PA in adolescents with a lower and higher social position. Researchers should be encouraged to systematically study effects of PA interventions separately in lower and higher SES groups in the future. This would lead to the confirmation of the findings in the present manuscript or to potentially discover interventions that widen or narrow social inequalities in teenagers.I. title: Changes in population cholesterol concentrations and other cardiovascular risk factor levels after five years ofthe non-communicable disease intervention programme in Mauritius
abstract: Objective-To study changes in the prevalence of risk factors for cardiovascular disease after a five year population-wide intervention programme pro- moting a healthy lifestyle in a developing country. Design-Cross sectional cluster surveys in 1987 and 1992. Methodology included a two hour 75 g oral glucose tolerance test, measurement of body mass index, waist:hip ratio, basal lipid concentrations, and blood pressure; and a lifestyle questionnaire. Setting-Mauritius, in the Indian Ocean. Subjects-All adults aged 25-74 years residing in geographically defined clusters. Main outcome measures-Age standardised prevalence of categorical disease and risk factor conditions and mean levels and frequency distribu- tions ofcontinuous variables. Results-Response rates were 86-2% (5080/5892)    in 1987 and 89*5% (5162/5770) in 1992. Significant decreases were found in the prevalence of hypertension (15.0% to 12-1% in men and 12*40/ to 10-9% in women); cigarette smoking (58.2% to 47.2% and 6-9"/o to 3-7% respectively); and heavy alcohol consumption (38.2% to 14-40/ and 2-60/ to 0-60/ respectively). Moderate leisure physical activity increased from 16-9@/o to 22*1% in men and from 1*3% to 2*7% in women. Mean population serum total cholesterol concentration fell appreciably from 5.5 5 mmoVi to 4*7 mmol/l (P<0.001). The prevalence of overweight or obesity increased, and the rates of glucose intolerance changed little. The population frequency distributions of blood pressure, serum lipid concentration, and a composite risk factor score shifted advantageously. Conclusions-Lifestyle intervention projects can be implemented and have positive effects in developing countries. A pronounced improvement in the population lipid profile in Mauritius was probably related to a change in the saturated fat content of a widely used cooking oil.

main_text: Introduction The multiethnic island nation of Mauritius in the Indian Ocean has experienced rapid industrialisation and general improvement in living standards over the past two decades, and its gross national product per head ofUS$2410 (Z 1607) in 1991 is at the upper end of the range for developing countries.' Its mortality, however, both for coronary heart disease and for diabetes mellitus exceeds that of most Western countries. 2  Alarmed by its country's high and increasing rates of cardiovascular disease, the government of Mauritius instituted a national non-communicable disease inter- vention programme in 1987, after a baseline survey of the distribution of disease and risk factors, which confirmed that the prevalence of non-insulin dependent diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and coronary heart disease was indeed high.-A dedicated non-communicable disease unit was created within the Ministry of Health, and since 1988 this unit has coordinated an intensive national programme aimed at modifying levels of risk factors related to lifestyle, including glucose intolerance, hypertension, hyper- lipidaemia, obesity, cigarette smoking, alcohol misuse, and physical inactivity. Primary prevention components of the programme have included extensive use of the mass media; fiscal and legislative measures; and widespread community, school, and workplace health education activities. All these components have promoted healthy nutrition, increased exercise, smoking cessation, and reduction in alcohol intake. The Mauritian programme is a demonstration project within the framework of the World Health Organisation's "Interhealth" initiative. 7 Mortality from cardiovascular disease has decreased in several Western countries over the past 30 years, and at least some of the decline has been ascribed to reductions in risk factor levels.&'0 The Finnish North Karelia project showed that population interventions can favourably influence cardiovascular risk factor levels and disease outcomes," but there are no compar- able data from less developed countries. In 1992, five years after the start of the Mauritius programme, we performed a follow up study to evaluate the effects of the lifestyle intervention programme.Subjects and methodsSAMPLING AND RESPONSE The eligible population for the 1987 survey comprised all adults aged 25-74 years living in 10 randomly selected population clusters, plus an 11th area, which we deliberately selected for its geographic concentration of the minority Chinese community. 3  The overall response rate was 86-2% (5080/58932. These eligibility criteria also applied for the 1992 survey (response rate 86&6% (2366/2732) in men and 92-0% (2796/3038) in women). We also studied age stratified samples from three independent population clusters in 1992. We selected each cluster deliberately to represent one of the main ethnic communities in Mauritius. Response in the independent sample was 81-9% (605/739) in men and 93-3% (696/746) in women. Unless otherwise stated, data from the original 11 clusters only are reported. The ethnic breakdown of the random sample was close to national estimates (table I ). The inclusion of the 11th area (Chinatown) led to a deliberate over- inclusion of Chinese subjects. This had little effect on overall results for either 1987 or 1992, and comparisons reported here are for the 11 survey areas. The 1992 sample underrepresented the age group 25-34 years (table I), and hence age specific or age standardised estimates are reported.SURVEY PROCEDURE We performed the follow up study in April and May 1992. Methods were standardised to those in 1987, and in many instances the same staff took part. For each location we invited subjects to a central survey site where a fasting blood sample was collected: all except diabetic subjects receiving treatment had a two hour 75 g oral glucose tolerance test.34 Other procedures undertaken in both 1987 and 1992 included measure- ment of height, weight, waist and hip circumferences, and blood pressure; and administration of a short questionnaire." Blood pressure was measured twice in the right arm of seated subjects with cuffs and bladder size 40-42 cmx 12-14 cm.5 There were five principal blood pressure observers in 1987, and four in 1992, three of whom also participated in 1987. Observers were trained by using videotapes and double headed stetho- scopes. Hypertension was defined according to the WHO criteria as mean systolic pressure > 160 mm Hg or diastolic (phase V) pressure -95 mm Hg, or both, or self reported current use of antihypertensive drugs. Borderline hypertension was systolic pressure > 140 mm Hg but < 160 mm Hg, or diastolic pressure > 90 mm Hg but < 95 mm Hg. 12  We calculated body mass index as weight(kg)/ (height(m))2. We measured waist and hip circum- ferences twice and used the means to calculate the waist:hip ratio.4 Trained interviewers graded physical activity during leisure on a four point scale (1=sedentary, 2=light, 3=moderate, 4=heavy) as described previously.4 In these analyses we have combined the moderate and heavy categories.LABORATORY METHODS We measured plasma glucose concentrations imme- diately after the collection of blood at the survey site in both 1987 and 1992 (YSI glucose analysers, Yellow Springs Instruments). Concentrations of total and high density lipoprotein cholesterol, fasting triglycerides, and uric acid were measured at the Central Laboratory, Mauritius, in fresh heparin plasma (1987) and serum (1992). External quality assurance on every 10th sample was undertaken in Newcastle upon Tyne. Methods remained constant at the Newcastle reference laboratory, which is a member of the Wellcome quality assurance scheme. In 1987 the Mauritius laboratory used manual enzymatic methods, but in 1992 it used a Chemistry Profile Analyser Model LS (Coultronics). On the basis of the external quality assurance data, Mauritian values for concentrations of both total cholesterol (1987) and triglycerides (1987 and 1992) were adjusted downwards by using calculated regres- sion equations.STATISTICAL ANALYSIS Prevalence was age standardised by the direct method, and 95% confidence limits for the difference in prevalence were calculated,'4 with the 1986 Mauritius population as the standard. A categorical risk factor score was computed which included components for glucose intolerance (0=normal, 1= impaired glucose tolerance or diabetes); blood pressure (0=normal, 1=borderline or hypertension); cigarette smoking (0=non-smoker or former smokers, 1= current smoker); and total cholesterol concentration (0-values <5-2 mmolIl, 1=values > 5-2 mmol/l). Component values were added, giving a score which ranged from 0 (low risk) to 4 (high risk). Data were analysed with the Statistical Package for the Social Sciences (spss)," Changes between 1987 and 1992 in categorical conditions were assessed with saturated log-linear models,'5 which included survey year, ethnic group (Indian, Creole, Chinese), and age (25-34, 35-54, 55-74) main effects, plus interaction terms. The importance of year in determining varia- tion in mean levels of continuous variables was investi- gated with t tests and analysis of variance, with ethnic group and age group (10 year strata) being controlled for.ResultsGLUCOSE INTOLERANCE AND HYPERTENSION Small, non-significant increases occurred in the prevalence of diabetes in both men and women, and the prevalence of impaired glucose tolerance declined in women. The changes between the two studies in the frequency of glucose intolerance were not inde- pendently important in log-linear models (table II ). The prevalence of hypertension decreased by 19-3% in men and by 12 -1% in women. Borderline hypertension also declined in both men and women, and the changes in the prevalence of hypertension were significant after age and ethnic group were controlled for (table II ). The decrease in hypertension was population-wide and occurred in all ethnic groups and most age groups. The distributions of both systolic (P<0-001) and diastolic (P=0-001) blood pressures shifted towards the left in both men and women (fig 1 ).OBESITY AND LIFESTYLE The prevalence of obesity or overweight, or both, TABLE n-Age standardisedprevalence ofnon-communicable diseases and riskfactors in Mauritians and difference in prevalence, 1987 *P value from log-linear model for change between surveys in prevalence, after age group, ethnicity, and interactions were controlled for. For glucose tolerance, blood pressure, and body mass index, three-level disease/risk factor variables (for example, obesity, overweight, normal) were modelled. -110 (-139to -8 1) 6-9 3-7 *P value from log-linear model for change between surveys in prevalence after age group, ethnicity, and interactions were controlled for. tMen n4 days/week; women 3 2 days/week. increased in men and women, and the year effect was independently important (table II ). Increases were seen in all ethnic groups and age groups. Similarly, Systolic blood pressure (mm Hg) C Diastolic blood pressure (mm Hg) mass index and .7 0.9 1.1 1.3 1.5 1.7 1.9 2.1 2. 3  High density lipoprotein cholesterol (mmol/l) mean waist:hip ratio and mean body mass index increased in all subgroups, and in analysis of variance the changes between the two surveys were independent of age and ethnic group (P<0001) in both sexes. There were shifts to the right in the cumulative frequency distributions of body mass index and waist: hip ratio (fig 1 ). The prevalence of cigarette smoking decreased in both men and women (table III ). These reductions occurred in all age groups. The proportion of Mauritians undertaking moderate or heavy leisure physical activity (aerobic activity lasting 30 minutes or more at least one day a week) increased from 16-9% to 22-1% in men, and from 1P3% to a still low 2-7% in women (table III ). The increases were seen in all age groups. Frequent alcohol consumption decreased in men from 18-2% to 144%, and this pattern was evident across all age groups and ethnic groups except Muslim Indian men. Alcohol consumption remained un- common in women. Similarly, the prevalence of heavy drinking (average 2 3 standard drinks a day) decreased in both men and women (table III ).SERUM CONCENTRATIONS OF LIPIDS AND URIC ACID Dramatic reductions occurred in the prevalence of hypercholesterolaemia and hypertriglyceridaemia in both sexes (table II); these changes were observed in all ethnic groups (not shown). The frequency distributions of concentrations of total cholesterol, fasting triglycerides, and uric acid each shifted towards the left, while high density lipoprotein cholesterol shifted towards the right (fig 2 ). Reductions occurred in the mean concentrations of fasting triglycerides (152 v 1P45 mmol/l), total cholesterol (5-52 v 4.75 mmoIl), and uric acid (351 v 319 pumol/l), and high density lipoprotein cholesterol increased (1 29 v 1-33 mmol/l) (P <0.001 for each comparison). Figures 3 and 4 show age specific data: the change between the two surveys was independently associated with mean concentrations of each variable except triglycerides in both men and women. Concentrations in the independent sample were similar to those seen in the main study areas, confirming that changes to these variables were population-wide.Discussion These data show predominantly favourable trends in risk factor levels for cardiovascular disease in Mauritius from 1987 to 1992, including a shift to the left in the frequency distribution of a composite risk factor score (table IV ).GLUCOSE INTOLERANCE AND HYPERTENSION The increase in the prevalence of diabetes is not surprising given the high prevalence of impaired glucose tolerance observed in the baseline survey3 and the well documented risk of progression to diabetes from impaired glucose tolerance. '6 We had no data to enable us to assess whether improvements occurred in survival that may have contributed to the increase in prevalence. In any case, the overall prevalence The increasing prevalence of diabetes reflects similar trends observed recently in other developing'718 and developed'9 populations. The decline in prevalence of hypertension is encouraging. It seems likely that a change in com- munity lifestyle (for example, diet) and improved case finding and medical care together play a part in the 420- 55.64 [65] [66] [67] [68] [69] [70] [71] [72] [73] [74] Age group (ears) Age group (years) FIG 3-Mean total and high density lipoprotein cholesterol, fasting triglycerides, and uric acid by age group in Mauritian men, 1987 and 1992. Significance levels from analysis of variance for change between two surveys in mean concentration of each variable, after age group, ethnicity, and interactions were controlledfor were: total cholesterol, P<O-OO1; high density lipoprotein cholesterol, P<O0OO1; triglycerides, P>0-05; and uric acid, P<O0OO1 Age group (years) FIG 4-Mean total and high density lipoprotein cholesterol, fasting triglycerides, and uric acid by age group in Mauritian women, 1987 and 1992. Significance levels from analysis ofvariancefor change between two surveys in mean concentration of each variable, after age group, ethnicity, and interactions were controlled for: total cholesterol, P<O-OO1; high density lipoprotein cholesterol, P<O-OO1; triglycerides, P>O 05; and uric acid, P<O0OO1 *Change between surveys in risk factor score, after age group, ethnicity, and interactions were controlled for, was P< 0-001 both in men and in women. decline. Certainly, the increase in leisure physical activity, the decline in alcohol consumption, and the beneficial changes in serum concentration oflipids may have favourably influenced blood pressure. Longi- tudinal data on salt consumption are not available, but the healthy lifestyle campaign also highlighted the need to reduce salt intake. Reductions in blood pressure have been observed in other population intervention programmes.11202' That decreases are also often observed in control cohorts2'122 suggests that part of the effect may be due to familiarity with the measurement procedure. Nevertheless, studies with independent samples at successive time points have also documented decreases in blood pressure both in the context of interventions20 21 and in whole populations,23 24 showing that there are true secular decreases, as well as intervention effects. In Mauritius the prevalence of hypertension in first time survey attenders in 1992 was similar to that in the 3751 cohort subjects seen in both 1987 and 1992, showing that a real decrease occurred in the population.OBESITY AND LIFESTYLE The interview data showed reductions in cigarette smoking and alcohol consumption, and increased leisure physical activity over the intervention period. National data on the sales of cigarettes and alcohol per head of population for 1986-92 support the survey findings (unpublished data). Decreased frequency of smoking has been observed in many developed countries over recent decades,9 1024 but generally smoking is common and increasing in the developing world. The Mauritian results are encouraging as they show that a comprehensive anti-tobacco campaign using the mass media, advertising bans, and increased taxation can be effective in a developing country. Of particular interest is that the uptake of smoking by Mauritian women has not increased. The increase in overweight and obesity contrasts with trends in other risk factors and is also at variance with the decreased prevalence of hypertension. Other studies have also noted either little change22 or increasing obesity25 despite favourable changes in other risk factors. Changing diet, the reduction in smoking,26 and declining occupational activity may play a part in the increase in obesity. Differing relative changes in specific dietary components might explain the opposing trends in obesity, hypertension, and cholesterol concentrations. An increasing prevalence of obesity has been documented in both developing27 and developed24 countries, and the trend in Mauritius is of concern.CONCENTRATIONS OF SERUM LIPIDS The improvement in the serum lipid profile was pronounced, particularly given the increase in obesity. The consistency of the changes across age groups, ethnic groups, and the 1992 independent sample suggests a population-wide phenomenon that cannot BMJ VOLUME 311  * Rates of cardiovascular disease and non- insulin dependent diabetes mellitus are increasing throughout the developing world, with little evidence that trends can be reversed * A national healthy lifestyle intervention pro- gramme in Mauritius included extensive use of the mass media, fiscal and legislative measures, and community health promotion activities * After five years there were reductions in population blood pressure and serum lipid concentrations, increased leisure exercise, and decreased smoking and alcohol consumption * A regulated change in the saturated fat content of a widely used cooking oil may have contributed importantly to the dramatic fall in serum cholesterol concentrations * Lifestyle intervention programmes can be implemented and have positive effects in developing countries be explained by chance or regression to the mean. External quality assurance data confirm that the changes are real. Intervention studies in Finland,"28; South Africa,22 and the United States29 showed reductions in total cholesterol concentration, although it was not clear whether changes in the intervention groups exceeded underlying secular trends. Certainly, evidence exists that population cholesterol concen- trations are declining in several developed countries.'428 Reductions in cholesterol concentrations and rates of coronary heart disease have been associated with declining consumption of animal fats.9-"'2429 A dramatic alteration in the average Mauritian diet would seem to be necessary for such clear changes in choles- terol concentrations to have occurred over five years. A sufficient change, however, seems possible. In 1987 most Mauritians used "ration oil" for cooking, which was inexpensive, sold in bulk, and comprised predominantly palm oil. Palm oil is high in saturated palmitic acid (16:0) and has been associated with high concentrations of total and low density lipoprotein cholesterol relative to polyunsaturated and mono- unsaturated oils.'0"1 After the baseline survey, the Mauritian Ministry of Health ordered a change in the formulation of the ration oil to almost 100% soybean oil. This change may have had an important effect on fatty acid intake and serum lipid concentrations. CONCLUSION It was not feasible to have a reference area in the small island state ofMauritius, and hence it is not clear to what extent the changes documented represent true intervention effects versus underlying secular trends. At the start of the intervention programme the Mauritian public had little knowledge of non- communicable diseases and their risk factors, but preliminary analyses show that their knowledge on health had improved substantially by 1992. The beneficial changes were probably in large part due to the healthy lifestyle intervention programme. These results show that lifestyle intervention projects can be implemented and have positive effects in rapidly developing countries. A pronounced improvement in the population lipid profile in Mauritius may have resulted from a change in the saturated fat content of a common cooking oil.(-1-4 to 2-4) 19-7 17-2 -2-5 (-4-6 to -0-4) Hypertension 15-0 12-1 -2.9 (-4-7 to -1 1) (3-0to6-6) P<0-O1 P<0-O1 Overweight (BMI 25-30 kg/ni)FiG 1 - 1 FiG 1-Cumulative frequency distributions for systolic and diastolic blood pressure, body waist:hip ratio in Mauritians, 1987 and 1992 100-Age group(years)TABLE s - s Ethnic group and age distribution of 1992 survey population (ages 25-74 years) compared with estimateforMauritius. title: '10 000 Steps Flanders': evaluation of the state-wide dissemination of a physical activity intervention in Flanders
abstract: The aim of the study was to evaluate the impact of the state-wide dissemination of a physical activity (PA) intervention in Flanders. In 2011, a random sample was taken of the entire adult (25-75 years) population of Flanders. Data of the Flemish sample were compared with baseline data of the intervention and control group of '10 000 Steps Ghent' (2005). In total, data of the International Physical Activity Questionnaire were available of 2556 respondents (1675 of the comparison sample and 881 of the Flemish sample). Pedometer data were obtained by 269 respondents of the Flemish sample and by 1236 respondents of the comparison sample. Compared with the comparison sample of 2005, the Flemish sample reported more walking (P < 0.001), moderate (P < 0.001), vigorous (P < 0.001), work-related (P < 0.001), leisure time (P ¼ 0.01) and household PA (P ¼ 0.03). Step count analyses revealed that the Flemish sample took more pedometer-based daily step counts (P < 0.001) than the comparison sample. Furthermore, a higher proportion of respondents reaching the 10 000 steps/day goal (P ¼ 0.005) was found in the Flemish sample. A positive effect of '10 000 Steps Flanders' was found. Results indicate that a state-wide approach based on socio-ecological models is an effective strategy to promote PA in a large population.
text: The aim of the study was to evaluate the impact of the state-wide dissemination of a physical activity (PA) intervention in Flanders. In 2011, a random sample was taken of the entire adult (25-75 years) population of Flanders. Data of the Flemish sample were compared with baseline data of the intervention and control group of '10 000 Steps Ghent' (2005). In total, data of the International Physical Activity Questionnaire were available of 2556 respondents (1675 of the comparison sample and 881 of the Flemish sample). Pedometer data were obtained by 269 respondents of the Flemish sample and by 1236 respondents of the comparison sample. Compared with the comparison sample of 2005, the Flemish sample reported more walking (P < 0.001), moderate (P < 0.001), vigorous (P < 0.001), work-related (P < 0.001), leisure time (P ¼ 0.01) and household PA (P ¼ 0.03). Step count analyses revealed that the Flemish sample took more pedometer-based daily step counts (P < 0.001) than the comparison sample. Furthermore, a higher proportion of respondents reaching the 10 000 steps/day goal (P ¼ 0.005) was found in the Flemish sample. A positive effect of '10 000 Steps Flanders' was found. Results indicate that a state-wide approach based on socio-ecological models is an effective strategy to promote PA in a large population.
main_text: Introduction Disease outcomes inversely related to regular physical activity (PA) include cardiovascular disease, obesity, type 2 diabetes mellitus and depression [1] . To promote and maintain well-being, the American College of Sports Medicine recommends at least 30 min of moderate to vigorous intensity PA per day in adults [1, 2] . This international PA guideline has been translated into a more practical step count guideline of 10 000 steps/day [3] . Using the step count goal of 10 000 steps/day, the Australian city of Rockhampton introduced the whole-community intervention project '10 000 Steps Rockhampton' [4] . The intervention was based on socio-ecological models and aimed to increase the levels of PA in adults by intervening at the individual, social and environmental level (see Fig. 1 ) [5, 6] . Later on, funding was provided by the Queensland government to implement the whole-community intervention on a larger scale [7] . Based on the above-mentioned approach, in Belgium, '10 000 Steps Ghent' (2005-06) served as a pilot for Europe, showing positive effects on PA behaviour [8] . As a result, in 2007, the Flemish government provided funding for the dissemination and implementation of the '10 000 Steps' project in the entire state of Flanders (Dutch speaking part of Belgium) [9] . In general, the dissemination of the program was carried out along the period of 2007 up till now by the various local health promotion services and partner associations [10] . This study purpose was to evaluate the effects of 4 years (2007-11) '10 000 Steps Flanders'. The pilot study in Ghent (±240 000 inhabitants) indicated that the use of a multiple community-based strategy was promising in promoting PA. However, evidence of the effectiveness of this state-wide PA intervention in a larger region is lacking. Therefore, the primary aim was to assess if '10 000 Steps Flanders' resulted in an overall increase in PA and in an increase in the number of people reaching the 10 000 steps goal within the Flemish population (±6 million inhabitants). An additional aim was to describe the awareness of the project within the Flemish population.Methods The intervention was implemented in the entire region of Flanders and consisted of several strategies at the different levels of the social ecologic model. The project aimed at increasing PA by intervening at the individual, social and environmental level (see Fig. 1 ). Identical methods were used to those of the pilot study in 2005. For a full description of the intervention development and study protocol, we refer to De Cocker et al. [8] . In 2001, making use of an electronic phone book, a random sample (n ¼ 1500) of the entire adult (25-75 years) population of Flanders was taken. The selection of respondents was balanced across the five provinces belonging to Flanders. Trained, native speaking interviewers contacted respondents by telephone and asked them to complete the Dutch long version of the International Physical Activity Questionnaire (IPAQ) and a questionnaire about awareness of the '10 000 Steps Flanders' project [e.g. Have you heard of the 10 000 Steps Flanders project? (yes/no); Where did you hear about 10 000 Steps Flanders? (radio, TV, print media, Internet, street signs, workplace); . . .]. Awareness of the project was assessed to gain insights into the reach of the Flemish intervention. Furthermore, potential differences in project awareness between both the Flemish and the comparison sample of 2005 could be explored. At the end of the interview, respondents were requested to wear a pedometer for seven consecutive days and to monitor their daily steps on a step count Evaluation of '10 000 Steps Flanders' log. In both samples, the same model of pedometer (Yamax Digiwalker SW-200) was used. Both the IPAQ and the Yamax Digiwalker SW-200 are valid and reliable instruments to assess PA [11, 12] . The study protocols were approved by the Ethics Committee of the Ghent University, and written informed consent was obtained from all subjects. A total of 881 respondents (59%) completed the telephone-administered IPAQ and 269 respondents (18%) wore the pedometer for seven consecutive days. Less than 1% of the respondents who wore a pedometer provided invalid data, from all others at least 3 days of pedometer records were available [13] . As '10 000 Steps Flanders' was implemented as a state-wide project in the entire region of Flanders, a historical control group was used. More specifically, baseline data of both the intervention and control city of the pilot study '10 000 Steps Ghent'-both cities belonging to the Flemish region-served as a comparison sample in present analyses. At baseline ( 2005 ), 1675 adults (89%) completed the IPAQ and from 1236 respondents (66%) objective pedometer data (7-day measurement period) were obtained. All data were analysed using SPSS 19.0 for Windows and an a level of P < 0.05 was used to decide upon statistical significance. Chi-square tests were used to compare both groups on the demographic variables gender (male/female), educational level (high/low education) and employment status (employed/ not employed). An independent samples t-test was used to assess possible differences in age (quantitative) in both groups. Differences in questionnaire-based PA and pedometer-based step counts were evaluated using univariate ANCOVAs. Evaluations were done for the total sample and for subgroups based on various individual characteristics, namely gender (men versus women) and age category [25 to 59-year olds (young) versus 60+ year olds (older)]. As the selfreported IPAQ scores were skewed, total IPAQ scores and domain-specific scores were first logtransformed to obtain normal distributions. For reasons of clarity, the non-transformed data were used to report mean, standard deviations, median and interquartile range in the table. To limit unrealistic high PA scores and step counts, mean values were truncated (see Table II ).Results To ascertain comparability between both the Flemish sample and the comparison sample of 2005, demographic characteristics were compared. For respondents providing self-reported PA data, no significant differences were noted between both groups in gender, age and educational level. Though, a significant difference was found for employment status. For respondents providing step V. Dubuy et al. count data, groups did not significantly differ in gender or educational level, however, respondents in the Flemish sample were somewhat younger and their employment rate was somewhat higher than in the comparison sample of 2005 (see Table I ). Therefore, further step count analyses were controlled for age and employment status. Analyses on self-reported PA data were controlled for employment status. Analyses of the IPAQ data (see Table II ) revealed that the Flemish sample showed a higher level of work-related, household and leisure time PA than the comparison sample of 2005. Overall, walking, moderate-and vigorous-intensity PA was higher in the Flemish sample than in the comparison sample. In contrast, respondents of the comparison sample of 2005 reported more transport-related PA than the Flemish sample. The step count analyses showed that the Flemish sample had significantly more pedometer-based daily step counts than the comparison sample (F ¼ 18.2, P < 0.001). Subgroup analyses revealed that this effect could be found for both men (F ¼ 7.7, P ¼ 0.006) and women (F ¼ 11.5, P ¼ 0.001), and both age categories ('young' age category 25-59 years: F ¼ 10.1, P ¼ 0.002; 'older' age category 60+ years: F ¼ 10.8, P ¼ 0.001) (see Table III ). Based on the average daily step counts, the proportion of respondents reaching the 10 000 steps/day goal was calculated. The data revealed a significant higher proportion of respondents reaching the 10 000 steps/day goal in the Flemish sample (50.9%) than in the comparison sample (41.5%) ( 2 ¼ 7.9, P ¼ 0.005). Looking at the data separately for gender and age category, the Flemish sample had, compared with the comparison group of 2005, a higher proportion of respondents reaching the 10 000 steps/day goal in males of the 'young' age group (58.3% versus 48.5%; 2 ¼ 3.7, P ¼ 0.05) and women of the 'older' age group (45.5% versus 22.0%; 2 ¼ 5.7, P ¼ 0.02). In the Flemish sample, the awareness of the '10 000 Steps Flanders' project was evaluated. About 61% reported that they already heard of the project, with a higher proportion of awareness among women (66%) than men (56%) ( 2 ¼ 10.7, P ¼ 0.001). Further analyses revealed no significant differences between those aware and those unaware of the program for step counts and PA, except for transport-related PA where those aware had significantly more transport-related PA than those unaware (t ¼ 2.3, P ¼ 0.02). Evaluation of '10 000 Steps Flanders' The most commonly cited sources of information were the print media, national TV and health services (community level). Looking further into the different levels of the social ecological approach, it was found that 18% of the Flemish population was aware of the sale of pedometers and 7% of the loan system (individual level). A total of 24% reported that they had used a pedometer. A higher proportion of pedometer use was found in women than in men (59% women, 41% men; 2 ¼ 5.5, P ¼ 0.02). About 10% of the Flemish population was aware of the walking circuits (community level).Discussion Results indicated that the state-wide '10 000 Steps' project in Flanders seems to be an effective program to increase PA at the large population level. A significant higher average daily step count in both men and women, and both age categories was found in the Flemish sample of 2011 compared with the comparison sample of 2005. In addition, self-reported PA levels were higher in the Flemish sample than in the comparison sample. Flemish respondents reported being more physically active in several domains (household, at work, leisure time) and intensities (walking, moderate and vigorousintensity PA) compared with the comparison sample. Furthermore, a significant higher proportion of respondents meeting the recommendation of 10 000 step/day was found, especially in male respondents of the 'young' age group (25-59 years) and women of the 'older' (60+ years) age group. Results concerning the project awareness revealed that several levels of the socio-ecologic model were mentioned by the respondents. In general, a high project awareness in the Flemish sample was found. These positive outcomes obtained in the entire Flemish region are in line with the results from the pilot study in 2005 [8] . Moreover, this study showed that these positive results can be replicated in a much wider area. Limitations of the current results are noted. Although to our knowledge no other state-wide PA interventions took place during the intervention period, possible influences of other concurrent local PA interventions cannot be ruled out, therefore, positive results cannot solely be attributed to '10 000 Steps Flanders'. Also important to keep in mind is that walking is the most common type of PA in adults [14] [15] [16] . The use of baseline data from the pilot study in 2005 is a second limitation. However, as '10 000 Steps Flanders' was implemented as a state-wide project in the entire region of Flanders, the use of a historical control group was the best option. Also, reference can be made to studies on the public smoking ban were similar comparisons relying on historical controls were used [17, 18] . Furthermore, making use of self-reported data, obtained by telephone, increases the likelihood of socially desirable answers. Also, the measurement properties of the IPAQ have been questioned [19] . Besides these limitations, a possible influence of the small proportion of the Flemish sample providing pedometer data requests cautious interpretation. The use of valid and reliable instruments such as pedometers is a strength, as well as the combination of self-reported and objective data on PA. Furthermore, this study contributes to a small [20] , few studies on the impact of state-wide PA interventions, using objective measures have been conducted [21] . In conclusion, this study suggests that a state-wide approach addressing different levels of the socio-ecological model used in the '10 000 Steps' program is an effective strategy to promote PA in a large population. An overall positive impact of the '10 000 Steps' project in Flanders was found. However, results on the step count goal indicate that younger (25-59 years) women and older (60+ years) men remain important population segments that should be focused upon. Further research on the use of different intervention strategies and goals for older adults of different gender is of interest when designing large-scale PA interventions.Fig. 1 . 1 Fig. 1. title: Determining the Impact of Walk Kansas: Applying a Team-Building Approach to Community Physical Activity Promotion
abstract: Background Research and practice partnerships have the potential to enhance the translation of research findings into practice. Purpose This paper describes such a partnership in the development of Walk Kansas (WK) and highlights individual and organizational level outcomes. Method Phase 1 examined: (a) the reach of WK, (b) physical activity changes, and (c) maintenance of physical activity changes 6 months after the program was completed. Phase 2 explored WK adoption and sustainability over 5 years. Results WK attracted a large number of participants who were more likely to be female, more active, and older than the adult population within the counties where they resided. Inactive or insufficiently active participants at baseline experienced significant increases in both moderate (p< 0.001) and vigorous (p<0.001) physical activity. A random selection of participants who were assessed 6 months postprogram did not demonstrate a significant decrease in moderate or vigorous activity between program completion and 6-month follow-up. The number of counties adopting the program increased across years, peaking at 97 in 2006 and demonstrated the sustainability of the WK over 5 years. Conclusions WK is effective, has a broad reach, and enables participants to maintain increased activity. It also shows promise for broad adoption and sustainability.
text: Background Research and practice partnerships have the potential to enhance the translation of research findings into practice. Purpose This paper describes such a partnership in the development of Walk Kansas (WK) and highlights individual and organizational level outcomes. Method Phase 1 examined: (a) the reach of WK, (b) physical activity changes, and (c) maintenance of physical activity changes 6 months after the program was completed. Phase 2 explored WK adoption and sustainability over 5 years. Results WK attracted a large number of participants who were more likely to be female, more active, and older than the adult population within the counties where they resided. Inactive or insufficiently active participants at baseline experienced significant increases in both moderate (p< 0.001) and vigorous (p<0.001) physical activity. A random selection of participants who were assessed 6 months postprogram did not demonstrate a significant decrease in moderate or vigorous activity between program completion and 6-month follow-up.
