main_text: Introduction The public health benefits of physical activity (PA) across age and demographic groups are well documented [1] [2] [3] [4] [5] [6] . Yet, only a minority of American adults meets the recommendations for PA [7] . In response, a number of interventions and strategies to promote regular PA have been developed. For example, the Task Force on Community Preventive Services (TFCPS) completed a review of more than 100 studies on different approaches to promoting regular PA [8] . Within these categories, the authors concluded that there was strong evidence for the effectiveness of communitywide campaigns, school-based physical education, and social support interventions delivered in communities. Despite the widely acknowledged benefits of regular PA, poor population prevalence, and two decades of research that have demonstrated the effectiveness for certain intervention strategies, there is little evidence suggesting that these strategies are being translated into regular practice. One of the reasons for a lack of translational and impact information could be the result of the type of research that has been used to evaluate evidence for PA interventions [9] . Flay [10] has identified that different types of research or methodologies are necessary to determine the efficacy, effectiveness, and dissemination potential for a given health promotion intervention. A number of recent review papers documented that almost universally, and across multiple contexts, PA interventions have been tested using efficacy trials and do not consistently report on issues related to generalizability and the potential for intervention translation into regular practice [11] [12] [13] [14] [15] . As a result, strong internal validity has been maximized, while issues of public health impact and translation have received less attention. There is a need to expand the research literature to include investigations that determine the effects of a PA intervention when implemented within community systems that have the potential to sustain programming once research support ceases [15] . Lessons from communitybased participatory research and diffusions of innovation theory can be utilized to complete these types of investigations. Specifically, a key component of participatory research is the collaboration between researchers and members of an organization that will ultimately deliver and sustain a PA intervention [16, 17] . The collaboration of research and practice benefits from the research knowledge related to evidence-based strategies and organizational understanding of what type of delivery and programming is feasible-a combination of understanding that heightens the potential for a program to be both effective and sustainable over time [17, 18] . Implicitly, participatory models move research beyond simple questions of effectiveness and towards broader outcomes that are highlighted within the seminal work of Everett Rogers [19] on diffusions of innovations. In his work, Rogers identified outcomes related to the design of the program and those related to the intended delivery system or organizational setting that could either facilitate or impede the spread of a given intervention [19] . Participatory models allow research endeavors to more explicitly address Rogerian principles to speed the dissemination and improve practicality when developing behavioral interventions. The Cooperative Extension System associated with Land Grant Institutions, and created by the Smith-Leaver Act in 1914, provides an excellent model for the combination of research and practice professionals [20] . The Cooperative Extension System is a statewide diffusion system that provides an infrastructure with the potential to adopt and deliver health promotion interventions that have a large reach into the population. The primary mission of the Cooperative Extension System is to disseminate and encourage the application of research-based information and programs to individuals, families, and communities. As it is available in every state, one of the major advantages of the Cooperative Extension System is its enormous reach into the American population [21] . Although the Cooperative Extension System was initially developed for the translation of agricultural research into practice [20] , many states have added health promotion as a systemic responsibility [21] . The general purpose of this paper was to describe and demonstrate the impact of Walk Kansas, a county-based PA program developed through a collaboration of researchers, state program specialists, and county agents responsible for program delivery within the Kansas State University Agricultural Experiment Station and Cooperative Extension Service in Kansas (i.e., K-State Research and Extension). The determination of impact was operationalized using the RE-AIM framework [9, 22] which highlights the need to evaluate the reach, effectiveness, adoption, implementation, and maintenance of an intervention to provide an examination of public health impact [22] . The specific purpose of this paper was to describe the development of Walk Kansas and present information on the individual level impact of the program (i.e., phase 1: reach, effectiveness, and maintenance) as well as organizational level impacts (i.e., phase 2: adoption and sustainability). The paper is presented in two phases that reflect the temporal sequence in which data were collected.General MethodsProgram Development The Walk Kansas participatory research team was organized to respond to the need to increase the rates of PA in rural Kansas [23] [24] [25] . The team was developed as a community-based participatory research group [17] and led by the K-State Research and Extension Health Specialist who held the decision-making authority to make health programs available for systemic implementation. The team also included a behavioral scientist with expertise in PA intervention development, a local county extension agent who would ultimately deliver the program, and community members. The participatory research team also made decisions on the project research questions, design, and interpretation of the project outcomes [26] . There was unanimous agreement among members of the participatory research team that it was necessary to base Walk Kansas on principles or strategies that demonstrated efficacy in previous PA trials and also upon information about feasible methods for implementation that could be derived from previous extension PA programs. Carron and Spink's [27] team-building model, the foundation of a number of PA interventions that demonstrated efficacy when tested using randomized controlled trials [27] [28] [29] [30] [31] [32] , was used as a basis for Walk Kansas. Table 1 is an overview of the evidence-based principles and strategies from this model that were used. To examine feasibility of implementation, two extension programs were identified that were based, at least in part, on the principles described in Carron and Spink's model. The first program "The Fitness Challenge" was developed by a county extension agent in Riley County, Kansas and focused on changes to fitness rather than moderate PA. The second program "Walk Across Texas" was developed by Texas Cooperative Extension System and focused on an Internet-supported walking program. Although neither program had extensive evaluation information, the participatory research team used these programs as guides for the potential structure and implementation of Walk Kansas. Walk Kansas program manuals were developed and training was offered to Cooperative Extension System agents from all 105 counties to facilitate adoption and consistent delivery of the program. The program was marketed to recruit teams of six individuals who would collectively walk the 423-mile distance across Kansas over an 8-week period. Participants could acquire miles through participation in any moderate intensity PA. With the exception of walking and jogging, which were documented as miles covered, 15 min of moderate or vigorous intensity PA was defined and reported as 1 mile [33] . Team size was determined to emphasize the current recommended guidelines for regular PA [2] . Thus, if six people were active for 30 min at a moderate intensity 5 days a week, for 8 weeks, they would meet the team goal of 423 miles. The participatory research team developed nine social cognitivetheory-driven newsletters that included fun messages, activities to encourage PA, and weekly team mileage updates. All materials were packaged in a program manual to facilitate ease of implementation. In response to criticisms of previous group-based PA programs and concerns of community members and agents on the research team, two primary innovations that were not components of previous efficacious team-building interventions were added to Walk Kansas. These criticisms were (1) when a group dissolves participant relapse may increase [34] and (2) getting participants to attend group sessions is difficult [35] . To overcome the first barrier, Walk Kansas participants were allowed to self-select teams from within their peer group (e.g., family, friends, coworkers) to increase the likelihood that participants' support structures for PA would be sustained beyond the conclusion of the 8week program. To overcome the second barrier, participants were allowed to be physically active on their own while retaining their feelings of responsibility to the group through team goal setting and feedback. Phase 1: The Individual Level Impact of Walk Kansas Phase 1 of the project addressed the individual level impact of Walk Kansas, and all data for this phase were collected in 2002, the initial year the program was offered. From a RE-AIM perspective, the purpose of phase 1 was threefold: (1) to determine the reach of Walk Kansas in the participating communities by assessing the number ofMethods Walk Kansas was advertised through flyers, newspaper advertisements, and public service announcements in the participating counties. The specific procedures that were used to assess reach, effectiveness, and maintenance are described below. Note that the samples were not independent and the linked nature of the data is depicted in Fig. 1 .Definitions and Measures Reach A number of researchers have emphasized the importance of determining the reach of health behavior interventions [22, 36, 37] . Reach was defined as the number, proportion, and representativeness of program participants when compared to the target population [15] . Number of participants was indicated by a count of registered participants, while proportion was assessed by computing the percent of the total county adult population that registered. Age, ethnicity, gender, and PA status were used to determine representativeness. All variables were assessed to be consistent with census and behavioral risk factor surveillance survey measures. Age was assessed on a continuous scale. Ethnicity was assessed on a scale that allowed the selection of Caucasian, African American, Latino, Asian, and Native American categories. Participants were also asked if their team was comprised of people from their worksite, community, family, or faith-based organization. Finally, participant gender was captured.Effectiveness and Maintenance The Center for Disease Control and Prevention Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance Survey questions were used to assess PA [38, 39] . Two questions were used to assess moderate activity: (a) "In a usual week, do you do moderate activities for at least 10 min at a time, such as brisk walking, bicycling, vacuuming, gardening, or anything else that causes small increases in breathing or heart rate?" and (b) "On days when you do moderate activities for at least 10 min at a time, how much total time per day do you spend doing these activities?" Two similar questions were used to assess vigorous activity. To determine the PA status, participants were divided into three categories. Those who reported that they did not do either moderate or vigorous PA in a usual week were classified as inactive. Those participants who reported doing moderate PA five or more days per week for at least 30 min per day or vigorous PA for at least 20 min per day on at least 3 days per week were classified as active [38] . Those who reported some moderate or vigorous activity in a given week but not enough to meet recommendations were classified as insufficiently active. Weekly minutes of moderate and vigorous intensity PA were used to assess effectiveness. This tool is valid and reliable for determining if respondents are active, insufficiently active, or inactive [39] .Procedures Reach All participants were asked to complete a paper and pencil registration form that included measures of the study variables. Participant responses were compiled by county of participation and then compared to county census data to determine any differences in participant age, gender, and ethnicity relative to their home county. As the prevalence of PA was unavailable for counties, the overall prevalence of participants reaching the recommended level of PA was computed for Walk Kansas and descriptively compared to Kansas Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance Survey data. Effectiveness Fifteen of the counties that indicated they would offer Walk Kansas were randomly selected and agreed to complete pre-and post-assessments of their Walk Kansas participants. All participants in each of these counties were asked to complete the pre-post evaluation. There were no specific inclusion or exclusion criteria for the participating counties, and 15 counties were selected to ensure that the amount of data collected was manageable based upon the evaluation and county resources available for the project. Those that participated in the study did not differ from the larger population of counties that participated in Walk Kansas on county size or demographics. Pretest surveys were included as part of participant registration and were completed before the initiation of Walk Kansas; posttest surveys were mailed to program participants with the final program newsletter, by the county extension agents, during the final week of the program. Maintenance A subset of Walk Kansas participants was randomly selected to complete a follow-up assessment. The number of participants was determined by completing a sample size calculation that documented 75 participants per inactive, insufficiently active, and active categorization would be necessary to detect a small to moderate effect.Participants Reach Participants from 48 counties registered for Walk Kansas in its initial year of delivery (2002) . The average population in participating counties was approximately 13,000 adults. All community members over the age of 18 were eligible to participate in the program (N=590,327). On average, 95% of the county residents were Caucasians, 3% were African American, and 1.5% were Latino. The median age of individuals in the participating counties was 39 years, and 51% were females. Effectiveness Participants (n=1,493) in the 15 randomly selected counties were eligible for participation in the effectiveness study (Fig. 1 ). Participants who joined Walk Kansas and were under 18 years of age (n=49), those who reported greater than 14 h of moderate and vigorous activity per week (i.e., >2 SD from the mean) at baseline (n=124) or follow-up (n=194), and those (n=47) that did not complete the baseline PA assessment were excluded. These exclusion criteria resulted in a sample of 1,045 individuals who participated in the effectiveness evaluation. Ninety-seven percent of the participants were Caucasian, 86% were women, and the average age was approximately 48 years. Ten percent of the participants were lost to follow-up (n=145). Maintenance We randomly selected 100 participants from each activity PA category with a goal of 225 participants. However, in the effectiveness study, only 78 participants were identified who were inactive at baseline. Hence, the random selection resulted in a total of 278 participants (Fig. 1 ). All participants were contacted 6 months after the completion of the program. We were unable to contact 28 potential participants, leaving 250 as the denominator used to determine participation rate. Of those, 42 declined participation (i.e., 16.8%), four were excluded because of missing data at any time point (i.e.,1.6%), and 14 were excluded for reporting more than 14 h of PA per week at any time point (i.e., 5.6%). The final study cohort included 190 participants (i.e., 76% participation rate). Within this final cohort, at baseline, 52 individuals were inactive, 71 individuals were insufficiently active, and 67 individuals were obtaining the recommended amount of PA. There were no significant differences in baseline activity level on participation rates across activity category. Ninety-five percent of the participants were Caucasian, 84% were women, and the average age was approximately 47 years.Analysis To analyze reach, comparisons of the Walk Kansas participants and the population of the counties where the program was offered were completed using independent sample t tests to determine if the program participants differed from the population of county adult residents-on ethnicity, gender, and age variables-within the counties where they resided. Because PA prevalence is only available at the state level [40] , simple comparisons of proportions rather than inferential statistical comparisons were completed. The effectiveness and maintenance of the program was determined by quantifying change in PA during the study period. Participants were categorized according to baseline PA status (inactive, insufficiently active, active), and change in PA was stratified by this variable. We tested whether the intervention had a similar effect among these subgroups using multilevel repeated measures analyses of variance (ANOVAs). Nested random effects were included to account for the clustered nature of the data (i.e., participants within teams, teams within counties). We adjusted the p values for the effectiveness and maintenance estimates to account for multiple pairwise comparisons using the Tukey-Kramer method.ResultsReach Of those who participated during the initial year of Walk Kansas (n=5,911), 78% completed the study questions on the registration forms (n=4,609). On average, 95% of the participants were Caucasians, 2% were African American, 1% were Native American, and 1% were Latino. The median age of participants was 46±15.8 years, and 81% were females. Walk Kansas participants represented 1% of the total population of the counties where the program was delivered. Female participants represented approximately 2% of Kansas's female population, while male participants reflected approximately half of a percent of Kansas's male population. Teams comprised people from the same worksite (52%), community (24%), family (12%), or church (4%). Ten percent of the sample was inactive, 33% was insufficiently active, and 57% was active before Walk Kansas. TheEffectiveness Walk Kansas participants from the 15 randomly selected counties increased minutes of moderate and vigorous activity between baseline and 8 weeks. For both moderate and vigorous minutes of activity, there was a significant interaction between time and baseline activity level after accounting for the influence of age, gender, and the clustering of teams within counties [moderate: F(2,1008)= 59.6, p < 0.001; vigorous: F(2,1008) = 12.4, p < 0.001]. Tukey-Kramer pairwise comparisons between participants at each baseline activity level indicated that the increase in moderate PA was significant for inactive (p<0.001) and insufficiently active (p<0.001) individuals, but was not significant for individuals who were already meeting PA guidelines at baseline. For minutes of vigorous activity, multiple pairwise comparisons revealed a significant increase between baseline and 8 weeks for individuals classified as inactive (p=0.005), insufficiently active (p< 0.001), and active (p=0.003) at baseline. These results are detailed in Table 2 . Specifically, previously inactive participants increased from no moderate or vigorous activity to an average of 172.85 (SE=15.0) and 45.49 min (SE=8.7) per week, respectively. Similarly, previously insufficiently active participants increase from 66.3 min (SE=2.0) of moderate and 4.4 min (SE=0.57) of vigorous PA to an average of 171.7 (SE=6.4) and 60.8 min (SE=5.3), respectively. Finally, participants who were active at baseline did not substantially increase their levels of moderate [214.Maintenance After controlling for gender, age, and the clustering of teams within counties, participants significantly increased minutes of moderate PA during the study period. The multilevel repeated measures ANOVA resulted in a significant interaction between PA status at baseline and time [F (4,372)=12.4, p<0.001]. Multiple pairwise comparisons using the Tukey-Kramer method revealed that participants increased minutes of moderate PA between baseline and 8 weeks. This increase was significant for those who were not meeting PA guidelines at baseline (inactive: p<0.001; insufficiently active: p<0.001). Minutes of moderate PA dipped between 8 weeks and 6 months follow-up for previously inactive and previously active individuals (not significant), while moderate minutes of activity remained stable from 8 weeks to 6 months for participants who were insufficiently active at baseline (Table 2 ). This pattern was similar for vigorous activity. After controlling for gender, age, and clustering of teams within counties, minutes of vigorous PA fluctuated during the study period as indicated by a significant interaction between baseline PA level and time [F(4,371)=4.5, p= 0.002]. Tukey-Kramer pairwise comparisons demonstrated that participants at all activity levels increased minutes of vigorous activity from baseline to 8 weeks, but after adjusting for covariates, the increase was only significant for the insufficiently active group (p=0.02). Participants who were inactive at baseline increased from no moderate or vigorous activity at baseline to an average of 203.33 (SE=24.2) moderate and 58.4 (SE=17.2)Phase 2: The Organizational Level Impact of Walk Kansas There is a dearth of information on organizational or delivery systems in their ability to uptake and deliver PA programs over an extended period of time [9, 12, 13, 15] . Program adoption and sustainability (i.e., continued delivery over time) are equally as important as the individual outcomes of effectiveness and reach [22, 36, 37] . For example, from a research perspective, one may conclude that if a program is not at least minimally effective, then it should not be disseminated. Similarly, from a community perspective, one may conclude that if a program cannot be disseminated (i.e., adopted and sustained) in the typical community context, then determining if it is effective is of little value. Rogers, in his seminal work on diffusions of innovations, identifies two influential categories of factors related to adoption and sustainability; those related to the design of the program and those related to the intended delivery system or organizational setting [19] . From the outset of program design, Walk Kansas was intended to be a program that could be sustained in the K-State Research and Extension system. The participatory research team increased the likelihood that the design of Walk Kansas would adhere to a number of Rogers concepts such as: (a) being compatible with system resources, values, and mission, (b) having observable outcomes (i.e., number of participants, changes in physical activity), and (c) including program resources for ease of implementation [19] . Similarly, the participatory research team also addressed organizational factors consistent with Rogerian theory. For example, the participatory research team included an organizational sponsor (i.e., K-State Research and Extension Health Specialist) with decision-making authority who could strategically foster adoption and sustainability. Further, the participatory team included an opinion leader for extension agents who acted as a strong program champion in marketing the initial program and the results of the individual level evaluation. Phase 2 of the evaluation of Walk Kansas was descriptive in nature and included two primary purposes. The first purpose was to determine the adoption of Walk Kansas in the participating communities by assessing the number of participating counties, the proportion of the target counties that adopted, and the representativeness of counties based on county level demographics and population size. The second purpose was to document the sustainability of Walk Kansas from 2002 through 2006.MethodsParticipating Counties Each of the 105 counties within Kansas was included in this study. Census data from 2002 were used to indicate county demographics including size of population. At that time, county populations ranged from 1,101 to 328,918 (μ=18,814; SD 47,461). Median figures from the counties within Kansas indicated that the residents were evenly distributed between genders (51% female), were primarily Caucasian (92.5%), and had a mean age of 39 years.Measures Adoption Adoption was defined as the number, proportion, and representativeness of counties that agreed to deliver Walk Kansas when compared to those that did not. Specific measures included the number of counties that adopted Walk Kansas each year and the percent of Kansas counties that participated. Counties were compared, based on census data, for size, average age, proportion female, and proportion minority residents. Sustainability Sustainability was operationalized as the number of counties that continued delivery after the initial implementation (i.e., two or more times). Further, members of the participatory research team indicated that from their perspective, the number of participants in the program across years would demonstrate a sustained interest from Kansas residents in the program and be an indicator of future program maintenance. Thus, we examined both the proportion of counties that delivered Walk Kansas for zero through 5 years and the number of participants per county across years as markers of current and future sustainability.Analysis The proportion of counties that adopted the Walk Kansas program each year from 2002 to 2006 was calculated. Counties that adopted Walk Kansas between 2002 and 2006 were compared to counties that did not adopt the program using census data specific to each county. One-way ANOVA was conducted comparing adopting and nonadopting counties on age, percent minority, and population size for each study year. To examine the existence of sustained population interest in the Walk Kansas, the reach of the program was assessed by summing the number of Walk Kansas participants in each county and calculating the proportion of county residents that participated. Proportional reach of county participants was assessed for study years using paired samples t tests which compared the total population of adopting counties to that of non-adopting counties. Finally, organizational-level program sustainability was computed by summing the number of years of participation for each county.ResultsAdoption Forty-eight of the 105 counties adopted Walk Kansas in its initial year. The number of counties increased across years, peaking at 97 in 2006 (Fig. 2 ). On average, the population of counties that adopted Walk Kansas in 2002 was approximately 18,760 compared to 18,860 for non-adopting counties. These numbers were similar from 2002 through 2004. In 2005 and 2006, counties that adopted Walk Kansas had higher populations than those that did not. For example, in 2006, adopting counties (n=97) had, on average, approximately 19,550 residents, while non-adopting counties (n=8) had approximately 9,870 residents. Although the difference is not statistically different, the disparity in county sizes can be explained by the presence of smaller counties that do not have full-time county agents assigned to the area and, as a result, do not offer the program. Similarly, the percent of minority residents of adopting and non-adopting counties did not differ in any of the 5 years that were examined. Counties that adopted, when compared to those that did not, were not significantly different on age. The yearly reach of county participants increased throughout the study period, demonstrating a sustained community interest in Walk Kansas. During the first year of the program, 5,911 individuals participated. Participation rates increased in subsequent years to 13,673, 16,816, 19,312, and 20 for at least four of five possible years (Fig. 2 ). A small number of counties adopted the program intermittently (N= 5, 4.8%), that is, program participation was disrupted for one or more years. Eight percent of counties were lateadopters, beginning program participation during 2005 or 2006. As depicted in Fig. 2 , the vast majority of counties that adopted Walk Kansas in a given year then continued to offer the program in subsequent years.Discussion Extension agents who would ultimately deliver the program and community members who were the intended audience were significantly involved in the process of developing, testing, and interpreting Walk Kansas. The process moved beyond the interplay between researcher and community member, as prescribed in participatory research models [26] , to include a more Rogerian [19] focus on organizational sponsors (i.e., those with decision-making authority for adoption and sustainability) and opinion leaders (i.e., potential delivery agents whose opinion is highly valued within the system) with the target of participatory dissemination of evidence-based principles into typical practice. By including all levels, from potential program participants to organizational sponsors, at the beginning of the planning process, the evidence from this project indicates that programs can be developed that will be attractive to community members (e.g., as evidenced by high reach) and attractive to those who deliver the program (e.g., as evidenced by high adoption and sustained delivery). Walk Kansas also reached a large number of individuals. Walk Kansas participants were representative of their county's general population with regard to ethnicity and age, but females were overrepresented compared to the general population. Walk Kansas participants were slightly older than the general population (46 vs. 39 years); however, participation was limited to adults age 18 and over, whereas the mean age of the general Kansas population includes children as well as adults. Considering this, we conclude that our participant population was likely closer in age to the general Kansas population than indicated by these numbers. It is noteworthy that the majority of participants reported meeting recommended guidelines for PA before the program began. Whether this is a bad thing (i.e., the program reaching those who do not need it) or a good thing (i.e., active participants assist less active through vicarious learning or social support) is difficult to determine from these data. What is clear is that nearly half of the participants reported not achieving the recommended guidelines at baseline and reported significant and sustained increases over time. On average, all Walk Kansas participants increased minutes of moderate and vigorous PA between baseline and 8 weeks, demonstrating the effectiveness of this program. This increase was significant for participants not meeting PA recommendations at baseline. The maintenance of Walk Kansas was made evident by the finding that on average, after 6 months, participants reported meeting or exceeding PA recommendations of 150 min per week. The adoption of Walk Kansas was a success. During the first year, 48 counties adopted the program. By year 5, 97 of the state's 105 counties (92%) had adopted Walk Kansas. Once adopted, these counties were able to sustain the program. Seventy-six percent of counties offered the program to its residents for four of the five study years. Previous PA interventions based on group dynamics relied upon creating new groups and providing group exercise sessions to develop a sense of cohesion and responsibility towards the group. Walk Kansas deviated from this model and allowed participants to join with groups of coworkers, family members, or friends and then allowed the participants to set a group goal without the need for group exercise sessions. Although there have been several walking interventions disseminated in the USA, Walk Kansas is novel because of this group-based structure that benefits from a participant's existing social ecology and creates the opportunity for regular PA to become a social norm [41] . The results of the effectiveness and maintenance projects provide indirect evidence that these adaptations were able to support PA change for insufficiently active participants and maintenance for those who were already active at baseline. We based the development of the program from a delivery perspective on diffusion theory [19] . That is, Walk Kansas was developed so that it was easy to explain and could be tested on a trial basis. For instance, some counties began the program within a small subset of the community to control the number of participants and practice delivery with a small audience. To enhance ease of delivery and reduce the complexity of delivering Walk Kansas, program manuals and training were also key components targeted during the development phase-both evidence-based approaches to dissemination and consistent with diffusion of innovations theory [19] . To provide a description of the relative advantage of Walk Kansas, after the initial year of delivery, presentations of the reach and effectiveness data were also provided to all county agents who were interested. Again, we did not systematically manipulate or test the potential mechanisms that could increase adoption and sustainability, but the rate and number of counties that adopted the program as well as the sustained delivery suggest that these aspects are of importance to a participatory dissemination model. Also of interest are the counties that have not adopted Walk Kansas. An important question is what groups are not being reached by the intervention in terms of those counties and the people living there. This may provide an indication of where these findings are not generalizable. Small counties without designated cooperative extension agents are likely also lacking other resources available to residents of larger counties. This issue could be addressed by expanding the efforts of surrounding counties to ensure the residents of very small counties have an opportunity to participate. However, further investigation is needed to determine the potential of this strategy to enhance the reach of the program to include residents of very small counties. This research included a number of limitations. First, because of the consistent implementation of key intervention components such as the use of teams, group goal setting, and feedback, we do not have a clear understanding of the components of the intervention that are critical and those that could be adapted over time and still result in an effective and sustainable program. Second, and related, there is a lack of process data to determine what may have mediated the programs effects, adoptability, and sustainability, thus leaving it unclear which aspects of the intervention were most important. This includes a lack of systematic evaluation of implementation as defined within the RE-AIM framework. Although all counties reported using the team approach, regular feedback, and resources for the program, the specific intervention content and timing of delivery is not known. This limitation is especially salient in light of the potential interaction between RE-AIM components and deserves increased focus in future investigations. Third, there were also a number of issues related to the internal validity of the study such as selection bias, simple and brief measures used, and potential attrition biases that may have influenced the individual level effectiveness and maintenance outcomes. Nonetheless, there were several strengths to the present series of investigations. First, this study used a participatory research model that allowed for delivery agents within a targeted system to design a study to provide the level of practice-based evidence that is compelling to those who will ultimately deliver the PA intervention. Second, this study evaluated a statewide strategy for adult PA promotion conducted in both rural and urban areas across several years. Third, the evaluated program was a novel group theorybased intervention that could be easily disseminated nationally. Fourth, in addition to examining the effectiveness of the intervention, the reach, adoption, and sustainability of the program were evaluated. Therefore, information not typically reported related to descriptive information of an intervention's potential for dissemination is available.3 min (SE=6.0) to 228.3 min (SE=6.2)] or vigorous [94.3 min (SE=4.3) to 111.3 min (SE=5.0)] PA.Fig. 2 2 Fig. 2 Number of counties offering Walk Kansas by year, representing adoption, and number of years the program was adopted by each county, representing sustainabilityTable 1 1 The principles and strategies of Walk Kansas , the proportion of the target population reached, and the representativeness of participants based on ethnicity, gender, and PA status; (2) to determine if Walk Kansas was able to increase PA in previously inactive or insufficiently active participants while maintaining the activity level of those who were active when beginning the program; and (3) to determine if Walk Kansas participants continued to engage in regular PA 6 months after the completion of the formal program. Evidence-based principle Walk Kansas strategy Develop a sense of group Unique self-determined team names distinctiveness Capitalize on existing perceptions of distinctiveness through self-selected teams of friends, family, or co-workers Walk Kansas t-shirts Group goal setting Cooperative goal rather than competitive Weekly individual goals to complete 30 min of moderate intensity physical activity on at least 5 days Group goal to figuratively walk 423 miles across Kansas Proximity Self-selection of team members to allow for geographic or emotional proximity Target recruitment locations where people already aggregate to benefit from pre existing proximity (e.g., worksites, faith-based institutions, community centers) Ongoing group interactions Weekly reporting of physical activity to team captain Promote occasional group 'get togethers' Self-selection of team members with preexisting and consistent interactions Communicate to provide Weekly newsletter feedback Weekly team updates within newsletter that provided current mileage of one's team relative to all others in the county Information sharing Weekly newsletter messages about healthy living, ideas for physical activity, and motivational tips Collective problem solving Promote group interaction to identify barriers to physical activity and solutions to overcome barriers ann. behav. med. (2008) 36:1-12 participantsproportion of Caucasian participants in Walk Kansas was representative in that the Caucasian participants in Walk Kansas were proportionate to the median percent of Caucasians within participating counties [94%; t(1,48)=.49, p=0.63]. When compared to the general population of the counties where the program was offered, Walk Kansas participants were more likely to be women [81% female; t (1,48)=38.2, p<0.001]. Similarly, Walk Kansas participants were, on average, older than the general population within the counties where the program was offered [46 vs. 39 years; t (1,48)=6.3, p<0.001]. When descriptively compared to state statistics, the prevalence of meeting the recommended guidelines of PA was higher for the Walk Kansas population (44% vs. 57%, respectively). Conversely, inactive individuals were underrepresented in Walk Kansas [10% vs. 19% (state)]. The prevalence of insufficiently active individuals participating in Walk Kansas seemed to be representative of Kansas residents (33% vs. 37%, respectively).Table 2 2 Effectiveness and Maintenance of the Walk Kansas Program: results from multilevel repeated measures ANOVA ann. behav. med.
